sources_audited.note: The cited Census page and its linked 2022 NAICS manual define 424610 as merchant wholesale distribution of plastics materials and resins and unsupported film, sheet, sheeting, rod, tube, and other basic forms and shapes.

The May 2024 OEWS table for the disclosed combined 4242-and-4246 group lists the stated sales, management, material-moving, stocking, customer-service, driving, and shipping/inventory occupations. It is not 424610-specific or wage weighted, as the packet acknowledges.

BLS lists prospecting, needs analysis, product selection, price and availability responses, negotiation, contracts, orders, follow-up, reporting, and administrative work, and says online sales mostly complement face-to-face selling while AI and chatbots may limit employment growth.

BLS describes supplier evaluation, price analysis, negotiation, contracts, ordering, inventory and sales-record review, and states that some procurement tasks will continue to automate or use AI while complex supply-chain and negotiation work remains.

The report verifies 2,575 establishments, 40.4 thousand employees, and $62.011 billion of 2024 shipments for 424610, plus the cited broad plastics-manufacturing history and 2025-2026 forecast. It does not directly support LMM eligibility, AI-savings retention, or five-year demand for 424610 distribution services.

The March 2026 ACC table reports 2025 plastics production of 136.123 billion pounds, up 0.1%, sales and captive use of 138.130 billion pounds, up 1.3%, and thermoplastic sales and captive use up 1.6%.

QTM describes material selection, processing guidance, on-site technical support, quality control, and just-in-time shipment, supporting the existence of value-added distributor workflows. A single company's marketing page does not establish their prevalence or quantify adoption, eligibility, retention, or operator need.

Curbell's April 14, 2026 announcement documents its acquisition of Interstate Advanced Materials, nine locations, more than 150 employees, continued key leaders and team members, and continuity of customer contacts and operations.

BizBuySell reports wholesale/distribution businesses sold nationally from 2021 through 2025 and a five-year median revenue of $1,378,849. The population is broad and much smaller than the stated LMM lens, and the page provides no plastics-distributor denominator.

Census reports that the share of wholesale-trade employment at firms with at least one-quarter of workers over 55 rose from 14% in 2000 to over 40% in 2022. This is workforce composition, not owner age, succession intent, or control-transfer incidence.

DOE states a 2030 plastics-innovation strategy covering deconstruction, upcycling, recyclable-by-design materials, and scale and deployment in a domestic plastics supply chain. Preservation of 424610 demand, added documentation burden, and a high operator-required share are reasonable but unmeasured extrapolations.

caveats: The a and rho magnitudes are judgmental transformations of a combined 4242-and-4246 occupation mix and broad occupational AI direction; no cited source measures a wage-weighted 424610 task share or five-year implementation rate.

S5 verifies the reported establishment and shipment statistics, but establishments are not firms and broad plastics-manufacturing growth is not five-year constant-quality demand for merchant-distribution services.
The q estimate has no direct evidence on pass-through of AI-enabled savings; the cited sources support sector context and service differentiation, not the 0.52 base retention fraction.

The s5 estimate lacks an eligible-firm denominator: one strategic acquisition establishes transferability, BizBuySell covers a broader and generally smaller market, and workforce age does not measure owner succession or sale intent.
S7 is a single distributor's self-description and therefore supports workflow existence, not population prevalence or the numerical bases used for rho, e, q, and o.
S11 establishes public goals for circular plastics technologies and supply chains but does not measure wholesaler demand or operator necessity; the d5 and o implications remain extrapolations.

Commodity resin distribution and value-added sheet, rod, tube, and specialty-material distribution may differ materially in labor exposure, retention, and operator need despite the packet marking the lens non-heterogeneous; the packet partly acknowledges this in its ranges and caveats.

The stated principal weakness says commodity transparency limits benefit retention even though the base C factor is capped at 10; physical-work constraints, reflected in H, are the actual binding weakness in the base score.
summary: Mechanics pass and every primitive-cited source was reopened. The sources support the registered industry definition, broad occupation mix and task direction, reported sector statistics, distributor-service examples, one observed acquisition, broad business-sale context, workforce-age context, and circular-plastics policy direction. Numerical primitives remain heavily judgmental and several sources support only broad or illustrative bridges, but those limitations are generally labeled PROXY or ESTIMATE and disclosed. Correcting or honestly downgrading the identified weaknesses would not change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action, so there is no material finding under the frozen test.
